data.title: Unilever Spin Offs Foods To McCormick And Refocuses On Health Care
data.description:
• Unilever (LSE:ULVR) has agreed to spin off its Foods business and combine it with McCormick.
  • The deal creates a combined Foods group valued at about US$20b as a global flavour-focused company.
  • Post transaction, Unilever is set to become a pureplay Health & Personal Care company.

For you as an investor, this move reshapes what LSE:ULVR represents. Unilever has long been known for a mix of food, nutrition, home care, and personal care brands. This deal concentrates its exposure in Health & Personal Care, while the Foods assets join a dedicated flavour specialist. The transaction takes place in a context where consumer groups are refining portfolios and focusing on fewer, larger categories.

Looking ahead, the key questions are likely to center on how the new pureplay profile affects earnings mix, risk exposure, and capital allocation options for LSE:ULVR. The combined Foods and flavour business with McCormick may also alter competitive dynamics in condiments, seasonings, and related categories, which investors may watch closely once more deal terms and integration plans are available.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Unilever by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Unilever.

📰 Beyond the headline: 2 risks and 5 things going right for Unilever that every investor should see.

The deal reshapes what you own through LSE:ULVR. Unilever Foods is being valued at an enterprise value of $44.8b, on 3.6x EV/Sales and 13.8x EV/EBITDA, with Unilever and its shareholders set to hold 65% of the combined food and flavour company plus receive $15.7b in cash. That is a mix of continued exposure to Knorr, Hellmann’s and other brands through McCormick equity, a cleaner balance sheet, and planned €6,000m of share buybacks at Unilever. The Reverse Morris Trust structure is intended to be tax efficient. However, you still have to weigh deal complexity, the long timetable to a targeted mid 2027 close, and execution risk on the planned $600m of annual cost synergies. Against peers such as Procter & Gamble, L’Oréal and Colgate-Palmolive, Unilever is moving closer to a focused Health & Personal Care profile. The market may assess it against a different peer set once Foods is fully deconsolidated.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Unilever Narrative

  • The separation of Foods and focus on Beauty, Wellbeing, Personal Care and Home Care line up with the existing narrative around portfolio streamlining into higher margin categories.
  • Exiting a large cash-generating foods arm could increase reliance on fewer segments, which may sit uncomfortably with earlier assumptions that broad category diversification supported more stable earnings.
  • The move into a tax efficient Reverse Morris Trust, a 65% stake in a separate NYSE listed food group and a long integration period for the $600m synergy plan are complex elements that may not be fully reflected in earlier narrative assumptions.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Unilever to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Unilever will lose the diversification and cash flows of Foods, which could make group earnings more sensitive to trends and competition in beauty and personal care categories.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have already highlighted concerns about deal complexity and leverage in the combined food company, and Unilever shareholders will carry exposure to those risks via their 65% equity stake.
  • 🎁 Unilever plans to use the $15.7b cash proceeds to cover separation costs, pay down debt to about 2.0x net debt to EBITDA and fund €6,000m of buybacks from 2026 to 2029, which could reshape the capital structure and share count.
  • 🎁 The combined Foods and McCormick group targets about $600m of annual cost synergies, with an extra $100m of synergies reinvested, which, if achieved, could support profitability at the food entity that Unilever investors will partly own.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, focus on closing risks such as regulatory approvals, McCormick shareholder voting and Works Council consultations, as well as any updates to the deal terms or cash and equity mix. Management commentary on Unilever’s post transaction earnings mix, capital allocation priorities after deleveraging, and dividend and buyback plans will be central for income focused holders. It is also worth tracking how peers such as Procter & Gamble, L’Oréal and Colgate-Palmolive are valued relative to Unilever once Foods is deconsolidated, and whether analysts adjust risk assessments, including the 2 flagged company risks, as more detail on the pureplay Health & Personal Care profile emerges.

data.title: Nike (NKE) Q3 Margin Compression Keeps Bearish Narratives In Play
data.description:
Advertisement

Q3 2026 headline results set the stage

Nike (NKE) just posted Q3 2026 results with revenue of US$11.3b and basic EPS of US$0.35, putting fresh numbers on the table for investors tracking the story through a softer margin patch. Over recent quarters, revenue has moved in a tight band between US$11.1b and US$12.4b, while quarterly EPS has ranged from US$0.14 to US$0.78. This gives a clear view of how earnings have shifted around a relatively stable top line. With trailing twelve month EPS at US$1.52 on US$46.5b of revenue, the key question now is how much of the current margin pressure proves temporary versus structural.

See our full analysis for NIKE.

With the headline numbers in place, the next step is to line them up against the most common market views on Nike to see which narratives still hold and which are starting to look out of date.

See what the community is saying about NIKE

Margins Under Pressure On TTM Basis

  • Trailing net profit margin is 4.8% compared with 9.4% a year earlier, while trailing 12 month net income sits at US$2.3b on US$46.5b of revenue.
  • Bears argue that margin compression could persist, and the drop from 9.4% to 4.8% supports that concern, yet
    • quarterly net income over the last six periods has moved between US$211 million and US$1.2b, which shows Nike can still produce much higher profitability in some quarters than the latest trailing margin suggests.
    • the consensus narrative expects profit margins to move from 5.4% to 10.0% over the next three years, so the current 4.8% trailing margin sits below that path and gives bears concrete numbers to point to.
On these figures, skeptics see plenty of room for further margin strain before any recovery shows through in the reported numbers, which keeps the bearish case in focus for now. 🐻 NIKE Bear Case

Valuation Stretched Versus DCF

  • Nike trades at a P/E of 29.4x and a share price of US$44.63, compared with a DCF fair value of US$39.98 and a single analyst price target of US$67.09.
  • Supporters of the bullish view say earnings growth near 20.4% per year can justify a richer multiple, yet
    • the current P/E of 29.4x is already above the US Luxury industry average of 18.9x, so investors are paying a premium compared with the sector even before any future growth is delivered.
    • the DCF fair value of US$39.98 sits below the current share price, which challenges the idea that the stock is cheap based purely on long term cash flow expectations, even though the analyst target of US$67.09 leaves room if those growth assumptions play out.
For anyone weighing the optimistic case, the key question is whether the growth assumed in those forecasts is strong enough to support a premium to both DCF fair value and the wider industry. 🐂 NIKE Bull Case

Earnings Trend And Dividend Tension

  • Trailing 12 month EPS is US$1.52 versus a five year earnings trend that declined at about 11.1% per year, while the dividend yield of 3.67% is flagged as not well covered by earnings or free cash flow.
  • Consensus narrative points to earnings potentially rising to US$5.2b over time, but current data raise questions around that path because
    • trailing net income has fallen from US$4.9b to US$2.3b over the last reported comparison, which sits at odds with the idea of a smooth climb toward higher earnings.
    • a dividend that is not well covered by either earnings or free cash flow puts extra pressure on future cash generation, since any slower than expected improvement in profitability could force a rethink of how much cash is returned to shareholders.

data.title: Is It Time To Reconsider Wingstop (WING) After The Recent Share Price Slump?
data.description:
• Investors may be wondering whether Wingstop's share price now reflects fair value, or if the recent pullback is opening up a potential opportunity for patient investors.
  • Wingstop's stock closed at US$144.87, with returns of a 12.9% decline over 7 days, a 39.7% decline over 30 days, a 43.6% decline year to date, a 38.3% decline over 1 year, an 18.2% decline over 3 years, and an 11.4% gain over 5 years, so recent performance looks very different depending on the timeframe you focus on.
  • These moves have come alongside ongoing attention on the business model, capital allocation, and how the current price lines up with expectations for the brand and its store base. While short term headlines can swing sentiment, the key question for you is whether the share price now lines up with what the underlying fundamentals might justify over time.
  • Wingstop currently has a value score of 3/6, which suggests the market may be pricing some parts of the business differently depending on the metric. Next up is a look at traditional valuation methods and, finally, a broader way to think about what the stock might be worth.

Find out why Wingstop's -38.3% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Wingstop Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a business might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today, so you can compare that value with the current share price.

For Wingstop, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flows in US$. The latest twelve month free cash flow is reported at about $98.34 million. Analysts provide explicit forecasts for the early years, and Simply Wall St then extends those forecasts further out. Under these assumptions, projected free cash flow reaches about $356.96 million in 2030, with a full set of discounted projections running through 2035.

When all of those future cash flows are discounted back to today, the estimated intrinsic value from this DCF comes out at roughly $239.12 per share. At the recent share price of $144.87, this suggests an intrinsic discount of 39.4%, indicating that Wingstop is trading at a material gap to this cash flow based estimate.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Wingstop.

Approach 2: Wingstop Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to connect what you pay for each share with the earnings that support that price. It lets you compare how the market values one stream of earnings against another across similar businesses.

Growth expectations and risk tend to shape what looks like a normal or fair P/E. Faster, more predictable earnings growth often supports a higher multiple, while more uncertainty usually calls for a lower one.

Wingstop currently trades on a P/E of 22.85x. That sits close to the Hospitality industry average P/E of 21.17x, and well below the peer group average of 80.13x. This indicates that some peers trade on much richer earnings multiples. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Wingstop is 17.31x, which is a proprietary estimate of the P/E that might be reasonable given factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and specific risks.

This Fair Ratio is often more useful than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for those company specific features rather than assuming all Hospitality names deserve the same P/E.

Comparing the Fair Ratio of 17.31x with the current P/E of 22.85x suggests Wingstop is trading above this earnings based reference point.

data.title: Knaus Tabbert Q3 Loss Of €14.7 Million Tests Earnings Recovery Narratives
data.description:
Knaus Tabbert (XTRA:KTA) has just posted another tough set of FY 2025 numbers, with Q3 revenue of €190.8 million and a net loss of €14.7 million, following Q2 revenue of €278.8 million and a marginal net loss of €0.1 million alongside basic EPS of €0.01 loss per share. The company has seen revenue move between €296.2 million in Q1 2025 and €195.6 million in Q4 2024, while basic EPS has ranged from €0.44 loss per share in Q1 2025 to €4.88 loss per share in Q4 2024. This sets the scene for investors to focus on how much of the current earnings pressure reflects temporary margin compression versus more persistent profitability challenges.

See our full analysis for Knaus Tabbert.

With the latest figures on the table, the next step is to see how these results line up with the prevailing market narratives around Knaus Tabbert’s growth prospects, risks, and potential path back to stronger margins.

See what the community is saying about Knaus Tabbert

Advertisement

Losses still heavy at €70.1 million over the last year

  • On a trailing twelve month basis to Q3 2025, Knaus Tabbert generated €961.4 million of revenue but recorded a net loss of €70.1 million, compared with quarterly losses ranging from €4.7 million in Q1 2025 to €50.6 million in Q4 2024.
  • Bears argue that persistent losses and five year loss growth of about 39.3% per year leave little evidence of an earnings turnaround. However, the recent quarterly pattern, with Q3 2025 revenue of €190.8 million and a €14.7 million loss, sits between the much weaker Q4 2024 and the lighter loss of €0.1 million in Q2 2025, which partly challenges the view that profitability is stuck on a single negative trajectory.
    • Bearish narratives highlight that the trailing twelve month net loss of €70.1 million is still larger than the quarterly losses seen in early FY 2025, suggesting that multi year pressure on margins remains a key issue even as some quarters look less severe than Q4 2024.
    • At the same time, the shift from a €50.6 million loss in Q4 2024 to losses of €4.7 million in Q1 2025 and €0.1 million in Q2 2025 offers a data point that does not fully align with the idea that operational measures are failing to have any impact at all on earnings.
Bears who focus on the widening multi year loss trend may want to test that view against how individual quarters are evolving in more detail in the 🐻 Knaus Tabbert Bear Case

Revenue near €1 billion, but forecasts point to only 5.6% annual growth

  • Trailing twelve month revenue to Q3 2025 sits at €961.4 million, and analysts currently forecast revenue growth of 5.6% per year, which is slightly below the 6.1% annual forecast for the wider German market.
  • Consensus narrative suggests product refreshes and portfolio simplification can support steady top line progress. However, the forecast 5.6% annual revenue growth and recent quarterly revenue swings between €296.2 million in Q1 2025 and €190.8 million in Q3 2025 show a profile that is more modest than some bullish expectations for above market expansion.
    • Supporters of the bullish view point to themes like premiumisation and demographic tailwinds, but the current forecast still places Knaus Tabbert a little behind the projected 6.1% market growth rate rather than comfortably ahead of it.

data.title: JFB Construction Holdings (JFB) Net Loss Deepens Challenging Bullish Narratives
data.description:
JFB Construction Holdings (JFB) has just posted another tough set of numbers for FY 2025, with Q3 revenue of US$5.0 million and a basic EPS loss of US$0.05, set against a trailing 12 month revenue decline of 20.4% and no net profit over the period. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$7.1 million in Q4 2024 to US$5.9 million in Q1 2025, US$3.7 million in Q2 2025, and US$5.0 million in Q3 2025. EPS shifted from a modest profit of US$0.02 per share in Q4 2024 to small gains in Q1 2025, then slipped into losses of US$0.13 and US$0.05 per share in Q2 and Q3 respectively. For investors, the latest release points to pressured margins and a business still working through loss making conditions.

See our full analysis for JFB Construction Holdings.

With the headline results on the table, the next step is to set these figures against the most widely held narratives about JFB to see which views the numbers support and which they call into question.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

TTM revenue at US$21.7 million with 20.4% annual decline

  • On a trailing 12 month basis to Q3 2025, JFB booked US$21.7 million in revenue, compared with US$27.3 million a year earlier, which lines up with the 20.4% revenue decline highlighted in the risk summary.
  • Bears focus on that 20.4% revenue drop and current losses, and the quarterly pattern does give them concrete talking points:
    • Quarterly revenue moved from US$7.9 million in Q3 2024 to a range of about US$4.0 million to US$7.1 million over subsequent quarters, then to US$5.0 million in Q3 2025, which points to a smaller revenue base than a year ago.
    • Over the same trailing period, net income swung from US$1.5 million in Q3 2024 to a loss of US$3.1 million by Q3 2025, so the softer top line is sitting alongside an absence of net profit.

Net income swings from US$1.5 million profit to US$3.1 million loss

  • Across the trailing 12 months to Q3 2025, net income moved from a profit of US$1.5 million in Q3 2024 to a loss of US$3.1 million, while basic EPS shifted from US$0.10 to a loss of US$0.17 per share over the same window.
  • Critics highlight this shift in profitability as a core bearish argument, and the quarterly figures give clear support for that concern:
    • In the last five reported quarters, JFB has only shown small quarterly profits of about US$0.03 million to US$0.35 million in Q2 and Q4 2024 and Q1 2025, compared with losses of US$0.37 million, US$2.4 million and US$1.1 million in the other periods.
    • The latest Q2 and Q3 2025 losses, at US$2.4 million and US$1.1 million respectively, sit against lower revenue quarters of US$3.7 million and US$5.0 million, so bears see a business that has not yet matched its cost base to its current revenue level.
Skeptical investors often ask whether this earnings profile is the start of a longer loss making stretch or a temporary stumble, and the detailed bear case breaks down those concerns in more depth in the 🐻 JFB Construction Holdings Bear Case.

P/S of 6.9x sits between peers and wider construction group

  • JFB trades on a P/S of 6.9x, which the analysis flags as lower than a 19x peer group average but higher than the wider US Construction industry at 1.4x, so the shares sit between direct peers and the broader sector on this single valuation metric.
  • What stands out for bearish investors is how this valuation sits against the current fundamentals, given the company is unprofitable and has seen shareholder dilution:
    • The trailing 12 month numbers show no net profit and a US$3.1 million net loss, so any P/S comparison is being made on revenue alone rather than earnings support.
    • Shareholders also experienced dilution over the past year and the share price has been described as highly volatile over the last three months, which bears see as extra risk layered on top of the mixed valuation signal.

data.title: If You Believe In Cryptocurrency And Blockchain Stocks Then These Three Pivots Could Define The Next Cycle
data.description:
Crypto and blockchain stocks sit at the intersection of technology, finance, and changing market plumbing, which can be appealing when geopolitical tensions, energy prices, and bond yields keep shifting. With manufacturing data mixed across regions and input costs under pressure, many investors are looking beyond traditional sectors to themes tied to digital infrastructure and alternative transaction systems. Our Top Cryptocurrency and Blockchain Stocks screener filters this broad universe into focused, listed names that are directly linked to this trend. In this article, you will see 3 of the strongest contenders from that screener and why they stand out.

Advertisement

Cipher Digital (CIFR)

Overview: Cipher Digital develops and runs large scale data centers in the United States that are used primarily for Bitcoin mining today and are being adapted for high performance computing hosting, serving hyperscale tenants that need significant power and cooling capacity for AI and other compute intensive workloads.

Operations: Cipher Digital currently generates all of its US$223.9 million in revenue from Bitcoin mining operations in the United States.

Market Cap: US$5.21b

Cipher Digital is shifting from being a pure Bitcoin miner to an owner of long term AI and high performance computing infrastructure, with 15 year campus leases, a 600 MW pipeline for hyperscale tenants such as Google and AWS, and low cost power contracts that support margin potential. Analysts have highlighted the company’s revenue and earnings outlook, yet it remains unprofitable, trades at a high P/S multiple, and relies on external funding while spending heavily on new hardware and sites. Combined with share price volatility, insider selling and uncertainty around how quickly AI leases are taken up, this results in a higher risk stock that may appeal to investors who understand both the potential benefits and these pressure points.

Cipher Digital’s pivot from pure Bitcoin mining to long term AI and high performance computing hosting could be masking a much bigger story about risk, funding and contract quality. Start with the 1 key reward and 3 important warning signs (1 is major!) and see what might be hiding in plain sight.

Bitmine Immersion Technologies (BMNR)

Overview: Bitmine Immersion Technologies runs a blockchain focused business that centers on Ethereum and Bitcoin, providing consulting and advisory services, equipment leasing, hosting and power optimization, and digital asset treasury management while gradually stepping back from running its own mining sites. The company also sells mining equipment and increasingly positions itself around institutional grade Ethereum staking and broader digital asset ecosystem services.

Operations: Bitmine Immersion Technologies generates US$7.19 million in revenue from the cryptocurrency mining industry, all from the United States.

Market Cap: US$9.0b

Bitmine Immersion Technologies may appeal to investors who want direct exposure to Ethereum infrastructure, rather than just token price moves, through a business that is building out staking services and ecosystem support around a very large ETH treasury. Forecast earnings and revenue growth are both reported as strong, and a low P/B of 0.8x relative to the broader US software space suggests the market may be pricing in a lot of the current unprofitability and funding risk. At the same time, heavy reliance on external borrowing, recent shareholder dilution, an inexperienced management team and a crypto concentrated balance sheet make the situation complex and may warrant closer attention.

Bitmine Immersion Technologies is trying to turn a complex Ethereum focused balance sheet into an opportunity, but the real story sits in the funding mix, treasury risk and US$9.0b valuation relative to its US$7.19 million in revenue. Get the full picture in the 1 key reward and 2 important warning signs (1 is major!)

Galaxy Digital (GLXY)

Overview: Galaxy Digital is a diversified digital asset group that runs trading, lending, asset management, advisory and blockchain infrastructure services, alongside data centers, bitcoin mining, and a GalaxyOne platform that connects individual investors to both traditional and crypto markets.

Operations: Galaxy Digital generates about US$61.2 million in revenue primarily from its Digital Assets segment, with smaller contributions from Treasury and Corporate at roughly US$99.2 million and Data Centers at about US$7.2 million. The company reports all revenue out of Canada.

Market Cap: US$7.2b

Galaxy Digital provides access to institutional-grade crypto trading, asset management and staking services, and also ties in large-scale Helios data center capacity that links digital assets with AI and high performance computing demand. Forecast earnings and revenue growth, a low P/S of about 0.1x versus higher peer and industry levels, and a planned shift to profitability over the next few years have drawn attention from investors who focus on value plus growth potential. At the same time, reliance on higher-risk funding sources, dependence on a major tenant at Helios and exposure to swings in crypto volumes mean the risk profile is complex and may warrant a deeper look at how the business generates its revenue.

Galaxy Digital’s mix of crypto trading, asset management and data centers looks priced for caution with that low P/S. The real question is what the analysis report for Galaxy Digital reveals about where this setup could break either way.
